title: Let the audience see the danger
advice: Suspense grows when the audience understands a danger that the character has not yet discovered.
examplePrinciple: Give viewers information first, then control how long the character remains unaware.
suggestedAction: Restructure the scene to reveal the threat before the character enters, then cross-cut between the danger and the approach.

title: Compress progress through montage
advice: A montage should show meaningful change, not merely shorten a collection of similar actions.
examplePrinciple: Arrange contrasting images so each cut advances time, skill, cost, or emotional pressure.
suggestedAction: Replace repeated process scenes with a short montage in which every shot marks a distinct stage of progress.

title: Make the reaction the event
advice: When the important action is internal, the actor's changing expression can carry more story than dialogue.
examplePrinciple: A close-up gains power when the performer has a precise thought to play and enough silence for it to register.
suggestedAction: Remove the explanatory line, give the actor a clear private realization, and hold a close-up through the reaction.

title: Design the day around natural light
advice: Natural light is a creative plan, not the absence of a lighting plan.
examplePrinciple: Scout the sun path and motivate small adjustments from windows, practical lamps, and reflected light.
suggestedAction: Schedule the widest setup for the best daylight window, then shape close coverage with bounce and practical sources.

title: Sustain pressure in one take
advice: An unbroken take can trap the audience in real time, but only when blocking keeps changing the dramatic pressure.
examplePrinciple: Movement, eyelines, and distance can create internal edits without cutting the shot.
suggestedAction: Rehearse a single take with three clear shifts in power and let the camera reposition at each shift.

title: Use voiceover as counterpoint
advice: Voiceover earns its place when it adds a perspective the image cannot simply repeat.
examplePrinciple: Tension appears when narration and visible behavior reveal different versions of the character.
suggestedAction: Rewrite the narration to express what the character claims while the image exposes what they actually do.

title: Build the unseen world with sound
advice: Sound beyond the frame can imply danger, distance, and scale without explaining or displaying it.
examplePrinciple: Let a sound establish location and movement before showing its source, if the source is shown at all.
suggestedAction: Remove the expensive reveal and create a changing offscreen sound path that forces the character to react.

title: Choose a location that tells the story
advice: A strong location contributes obstacles, history, and visual behavior instead of serving as neutral background.
examplePrinciple: The best setting makes the scene harder, reveals the character, or changes how the action must unfold.
suggestedAction: Replace the generic room with a location whose layout and history force a revealing choice from the protagonist.

title: Turn the budget limit into a rule
advice: A low budget becomes useful when it creates one clear formal rule rather than many accidental compromises.
examplePrinciple: Concentrate money on the element the audience must believe and simplify everything that does not support it.
suggestedAction: Choose one production value to protect, combine minor locations, and rewrite coverage around the resources already secured.

title: Promise one clear audience experience
advice: A trailer should position the film around a specific emotional promise, not summarize every subplot.
examplePrinciple: Select moments that prove tone and stakes while preserving the question that makes viewers want the answer.
suggestedAction: Cut the trailer around one audience promise, introduce the central dilemma early, and withhold the final consequence.

title: Give the visual world a governing rule
advice: Production design should express the story's pressures through space, color, texture, and the objects characters use.
examplePrinciple: A repeated visual rule makes separate sets feel like one authored world and gives change dramatic meaning.
suggestedAction: Define one palette and material rule for the character's current world, then break it only at the story's turning point.

title: Let a motif change with the character
advice: A musical motif becomes storytelling when its rhythm, harmony, or instrumentation changes with the character.
examplePrinciple: Repeat identity, not identical music: preserve a recognizable phrase while transforming its meaning.
suggestedAction: Assign the protagonist a short motif, state it simply at the opening, and alter one musical element at each major turn.
